coredata.dc:description: Singlet oxygen (1O2) is a type of reactive oxygen species (ROS), playing a vital role in the physiological and pathophysiological processes. Specific probes for monitoring intracellular 1O2 still remain challenging. In this study, we develop a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the real-time intracellular detection of 1O2 using o-phenylenediamine-derived carbonized polymer dots (o-PD CPDs). The o-PD CPDs possessing dual-excitation-emission properties (blue and yellow fluorescence) were successfully synthesized in a two-phase system (water/acetonitrile) using an ionic liquid tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate as a supporting electrolyte through the electrolysis of o-PD. The o-PD CPDs can act as a photosensitizer to produce 1O2 upon white LED irradiation, in turn, the generated 1O2 selectively quenches the yellow emission of the o-PD CPDs. This quenching behavior is ascribed to the specific cycloaddition reaction between 1O2 and alkene groups in the polymer scaffolds on o-PD CPDs. The interior carbon core can be a reliable internal standard since its blue fluorescence intensity remains unchanged in the presence of 1O2. The ratiometric response of o-PD CPDs is selective toward 1O2 against other ROS species. The developed o-PD CPDs have been successfully applied to monitor the 1O2 level in the intracellular environment. Furthermore, in the inflammatory neutrophil cell model, o-PD CPDs can also detect the 1O2 and other ROS species such as hypochlorous acid after phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA)-induced inflammation. Through the dual-channel fluorescence imaging, the ratiometric response of o-PD CPDs shows great potential for detecting endogenous and stimulating 1O2 in vivo.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent imaging of inflammatory responses in neutrophil cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of o-PD CPDs synthesized by an electrochemical method. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of o-PDs (20 μg mL−1) and o-PD CPDs (20 μg mL−1) in PBS solution (pH 7.4). (B) Fluorescence spectra of o-PD CPDs with an excitation from 280 nm to 420 nm. (C) TEM image, (D) SEM image, and (E) HRTEM image of o-PD CPDs. Insets to (C) and (D) show the size distribution histograms of o-PD CPDs. The fluorescence intensities (I F) in (B) are plotted in arbitrary units (a.u.).

Photo-induced self-reactions of o-PD CPDs. (A) Fluorescence spectra of o-PD CPDs (20 μg mL−1) in PBS after different periods of irradiation time under white LED (60 mW cm−2). (B) ESR spectra, (C) TEM and (D) SEM images of o-PD CPDs after white LED irradiation (60 mW cm−2) for 30 min. The ESR spectra of TMP solutions (10 mM) upon reacting with o-PD CPDs (150 μg mL−1) or Rose bengal (2.0 μM) after white LED irradiation (60 mW cm−2) for 10 min. Inset to (A) shows the fluorescent photograph of o-PD CPDs solution after irradiation by LED light.

1O2 induced selective fluorescence quenching of o-PD CPDs. (A) Fluorescence spectra with the excitation at 330 or 420 nm and (B) relative fluorescence ratio change [(I F0 575/375 − I F 575/375)/I F0 575/375] of o-PD CPDs (20 μg mL−1) in PBS after being reacted with different concentrations of 1O2 (0 mM–2.0 mM) generated from NaClO (0–2.0 mM)/H2O2 (5 mM). The I F0 575/375 and I F 575/375 represent the fluorescence intensity ratios of o-PD CPDs at 575/375 nm with excitation wavelengths at 420/330 nm in the absence and presence of 1O2, respectively. (C) Fluorescence spectra and (D) relative fluorescence (I F/I F0) of o-PD CPDs (20 μg mL−1) in PBS at 375/575 nm in the presence of different ROS [H2O2 (2.0 mM), 1O2 (2.0 mM), NaClO (2.0 mM), ⋅OH (2.0 mM), and superoxide (⋅O2 –, 2.0 mM),] with an excitation of 330/420 nm. The I F0 and I F represent the fluorescence intensities of o-PD CPDs at 375/575 nm with excitation wavelengths at 330/420 nm in the absence and presence of ROS, respectively. Error bars in (B) and (D) represent standard deviations derived from five repeated experiments.

Detection of intracellular 1O2. (A) Bright-field, blue (λex 320–330 nm), yellow (λex 460–470 nm), and the ratio (yellow/blue) fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells stained with o-PD CPDs (50 μg mL−1), followed by treatment with H2O2 (2.0 mM)/NaClO (0–2.2 mM). (B) Plots of fluorescence ratio of yellow-to-blue against various intracellular concentrations of 1O2. I F-Y and I F-B represent the yellow and blue fluorescence intensities of the cell images, respectively. Scale bar in (A): 10 μm. Error bars in (B) represent standard deviations derived from five repeated experiments.

Fluorescence staining of PMA-induced 1O2 in neutrophil cells by o-PD CPDs; (A) cell images and (B) fluorescence intensities. (A) Bright-field, blue (λex 320–330 nm), yellow (λex 460–470 nm), and the ratio (yellow/blue) fluorescence images of neutrophil cells incubated with o-PD CPDs (50 μg mL−1), followed by treatment with PMA (0–50 nM). (B) Yellow (I F-Y) and blue (I F-B) fluorescence intensities of neutrophil cells after being treated with different concentrations of PMA. Error bars in (B) represent standard deviations derived from five repeated experiments. The morphology of neutrophil cells is shown in the inset.
Schematic representation of the preparation of o-PD CPDs and its application in the sensing of intracellular 1O2.

head.ce:title: Dual-emissive carbonized polymer dots for the ratiometric fluorescence imaging of singlet oxygen in living cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Singlet oxygen (1O2) is a type of reactive oxygen species (ROS), playing a vital role in the physiological and pathophysiological processes. Specific probes for monitoring intracellular 1O2 still remain challenging. In this study, we develop a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the real-time intracellular detection of 1O2 using o-phenylenediamine-derived carbonized polymer dots (o-PD CPDs). The o-PD CPDs possessing dual-excitation-emission properties (blue and yellow fluorescence) were successfully synthesized in a two-phase system (water/acetonitrile) using an ionic liquid tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate as a supporting electrolyte through the electrolysis of o-PD. The o-PD CPDs can act as a photosensitizer to produce 1O2 upon white LED irradiation, in turn, the generated 1O2 selectively quenches the yellow emission of the o-PD CPDs. This quenching behavior is ascribed to the specific cycloaddition reaction between 1O2 and alkene groups in the polymer scaffolds on o-PD CPDs. The interior carbon core can be a reliable internal standard since its blue fluorescence intensity remains unchanged in the presence of 1O2. The ratiometric response of o-PD CPDs is selective toward 1O2 against other ROS species. The developed o-PD CPDs have been successfully applied to monitor the 1O2 level in the intracellular environment. Furthermore, in the inflammatory neutrophil cell model, o-PD CPDs can also detect the 1O2 and other ROS species such as hypochlorous acid after phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA)-induced inflammation. Through the dual-channel fluorescence imaging, the ratiometric response of o-PD CPDs shows great potential for detecting endogenous and stimulating 1O2 in vivo.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Singlet oxygen (1O2) has recently caught attention because of its high electrophilicity and excited-energy state [1]. In biosystems, a broad range of biomolecules such as nucleic acids, lipids, and proteins can react with 1O2 [2,3]. These reactions are involved in the key mechanisms to regulate the intracellular signaling networks such as inflammation, cell apoptosis, and mitochondrial DNA replication [4–7]. Thus, the presence of 1O2 is critically linked to human pathophysiology [4–7]. Though mainly produced in an endogenous pathway, 1O2 can be generated artificially for various applications, such as tumor therapy, wastewater purification, and even in organic synthesis to improve the production yield [8,9]. Despite significant advances, the 1O2-involved chemical reactions remain insufficiently understood due to their difficulties in monitoring. The short lifetime of 1O2 in living cells (τ 1/2 = 10−6 to 10−5 s) limits the diffusion distance, causing the detection task to be even more challenging [10,11]. Direct monitoring of the intrinsic phosphorescence of 1O2 lacks practical utility because of its extremely low quantum yield (ca. 10−6) [12]. Thus, there is a strong demand for developing 1O2 probes with high sensitivities and large dynamic ranges.

Over the years, numerous organic dye-based optical probes have been developed to monitor 1O2, which were mainly designed based on the strong enophile nature of 1O2 toward [2 + 2] or [2 + 4] cycloaddition reactions with double-bond containing structures [13–15]. However, several disadvantages still exist in most of the dyes, including water insolubility, poor photostability, and inefficient cell permeation [16–18]. Moreover, organic dyes are usually synthesized through complicated and time-consuming synthesis and purification processes [17–19]. Although some nanomaterial-based probes have been developed to detect reactive oxygen species (ROS) [20–25], they are rarely applied for sensing 1O2 with high selectivity [26,27].

Thanks to the simple synthesis, low-cost and robust chemical inertness; carbon dots (CDs), including carbon nanodots, carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), have recently become a rising star in the family of nanomaterials [28–34]. The optical properties of CDs are highly dependent on their core and edge states and their surface functional groups [35]. CDs have received wide scientific attention in cellular sensing and imaging due to their superior optical properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [28–34,36]. To the best of our knowledge, the use of fluorescent CDs for 1O2 detection has not been reported yet. Several approaches such as microwave-assisted, hydrothermal and solvothermal routes have been employed for the preparation of CDs [28–34]. Unlike those methods, electrochemical methods can be operated under mild conditions (ambient temperature and pressure) to avoid over-carbonization and improve the response toward specific analytes [37–39]. Moreover, the formation of CDs could be real-time monitored during the electrolysis, allowing to more easily optimize the synthetic parameters, including reaction time, voltage, and solvent [40]. Nevertheless, the electrochemical method still suffers from low production yields.

Herein, we report a simple method to synthesize fluorescent CPDs from o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) through an electrochemical process in a two-phase system (Scheme 1 ). Acetonitrile was used to increase the solubility of o-PD, and an ionic liquid, tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6), acted as an electrolyte to enhance the electrolysis efficiency. The as-formed o-PD CPDs exhibit two strong emission bands at 375 and 575 nm. The second fluorescence band selectively responded toward 1O2, mainly due to the oxidation of conjugated cycloalkene structures and amino groups on the surface of o-PD CPDs. The o-PD CPDs were employed for fluorescent ratiometric detection of 1O2 with great sensitivity and for bioimaging of intracellular 1O2.

The o-PD CPDs were electrochemically prepared using a bespoke apparatus (Kaixin Co. ltd, Taiwan) consisting of a voltage supply (10 V), and a 25-mL bottle with two platinum electrodes (length: 4.0 cm; diameter: 0.02 mm). o-PD (0.108 g, 0.1 M) was added into the 25-mL bottle containing two-phase solution with equal volumes (10 mL) of acetonitrile and water with TBAPF6 (0.382 g, 0.1 M). Upon turning on the power, the colorless solution gradually turned yellow, and finally brown, indicating the polymerization and partial carbonization of o-PD. Photographs and UV–vis spectra of the solutions were recorded every 10 min during a 60-min electrolysis process to investigate the formation dynamics. The as-formed o-PD CPDs were purified by centrifugation (8000 g, 10 min) to remove the bigger particles, the o-PD CPDs in the supernatant were further purified by dialysis (molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) = 0.5–1 kDa, Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) against deionized water for 10 h (replacing the water every 2 h) to remove unreacted precursors, inorganic ions, and degraded molecules. The o-PD CPDs were further purified by Amicon Ultra Centrifugal Filters (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany) with a MWCO of 3 kDa; the filtrate showed no obvious optical absorbance in the UV–vis region. The concentration of the purified o-PD CPDs was determined by vacuum drying method.

The H2O2‑NaClO system was used to generate 1O2 in this study. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; containing 137 mM NaCl, 5.0 mM KCl, 0.5 mM CaCl2, 1.0 mM MgCl2, 10 mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0 mM KH2PO4; pH 7.4) solution was used to prepare all solutions. For 1O2 sensing, 120 μL of o-PD CPDs solution (40 μg mL−1) was mixed with 60 μL of 20 mM H2O2, in which aliquots (60 μL) of NaClO (0–8 mM) were added to generate various concentrations of 1O2. After thoroughly mixing and incubating for 2 min, the solution was transferred into a 96-well plate for fluorescence measurement over the wavelength ranges of 310 to 700 nm under excitation wavelengths of 280 to 420 nm, separately. To investigate the selectivity of o-PD CPDs toward 1O2, 2.0 mM of other ROS, including H2O2, ClO−, ⋅OH, and ⋅O2 −, were tested. Their fluorescence intensities were recorded at 375 and 575 nm under excitation wavelengths of 330 and 420 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent imaging of inflammatory responses in neutrophil cells
ce:section.ce:para: The neutrophil cells were collected from rat blood according to a literature procedure [41], and used for bioimaging experiments on the same day. All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the institutional guidelines of Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of National Taiwan University. The operation permission is granted by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the National Laboratory Animal Center, Taipei, Taiwan (IACUC Approval No. IACUC2012-037). The neutrophil cells (1.0 × 105 cells) were seeded in a 24-well plate for 30 min of adhesion, and then pre-treated with o-PD CPDs (50 μg mL−1) for 1 h, followed by PBS washing for three times. Herein, the neutrophil cells were stimulated with different concentrations (10–50 nM) of phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA) for 1 h to induce inflammation, followed by PBS washing for three times. The fluorescence imaging of the cells was carried out using phase-contrast fluorescence microscopy (Olympus IX71, Tokyo, Japan) to observe the release of endogenous 1O2 by the neutrophil cells. Finally, each sample was transferred into a 96-well plate for the fluorescence measurement at 375 and 575 nm under excitation wavelengths of 330 and 420 nm, respectively.
The details on the materials, characterizations, photo-induced fluorescence quenching, cytotoxicity assay, and real-time cell imaging are provided in the Supporting Information.

The electrolysis of o-PD in a two-phase system (water/acetonitrile) at 10 V allows to prepare CPDs, mainly because the low-oxidation potential of o-PD (<0.7 V) made it prone to oxidation-mediated electropolymerization and partial carbonization [42,43]. The formation of o-PD CPDs in the ionic liquid (TBAPF6)-assisted two-phase solution may involve sequent steps of polymerization, carbonization, and passivation at the anode. According to the reported article [44], the electrochemical oxidation of o-PD may occur through the following steps ( Fig. S1 , Supporting Information): (i) the o-PD undergoes oxidation to yield a dimer, p-quinonediimine (PQDI) with an open ring structure, which further undergoes polymerization to form P(PQDI) [45]; (ii) the intramolecular addition of amine group in PQDI results in aromatization and formation of dihydrophenazine (DHP); and (iii) the hydrolysis of PQDI leads to the formation of p-quinoneimine (PQI). The various products formed in the above steps further undergo carbonization during electrolysis to form the CPDs. It is noteworthy that hydrolysis of o-PD induced oxygen incorporation can improve the aqueous solubility of o-PD CPDs. Therefore, the as-formed hydrophilic o-PD CPDs were mainly distributed in the water phase.

To confirm the interaction between tetrabutylammonium (TBA+) and o-PD, UV–vis absorption spectra of TBAPF6 alone, o-PD alone, and TBAPF6/o-PD mixed solution were recorded. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information), the peak for o-PD at 207 nm undergoes a slight red-shift (to 212 nm) and decreases in the presence of TBAPF6 compared to the sum spectrum, supporting the interaction between them. To prove accelerated electron transfer in ionic liquid (TBAPF6)-assisted electrolysis of o-PD in the two-phase system (water/acetonitrile), we compared the oxidation current of o-PD in three different electrolytes, including TBAPF6, NaCl, and (NH4)2SO4. The o-PD in TBAPF6 showed a higher oxidation peak current (1.4 mA) compared to that in NaCl (0.80 mA) and (NH4)2SO4 (0.75 mA) (Fig. S3 B, Supporting Information). Furthermore, after 60 cycles of cyclic voltammetry (CV), an obvious electrode poisoning was observed in the NaCl and (NH4)2SO4 electrolyte systems as a result of the adsorption of oxidized o-PD products on the electrode ( Fig. S4 , Supporting Information). By contrast, the oxidized o-PD products in the ionic liquid (TBAPF6) electrolyte tend to transfer into the aqueous phase. As shown in the time-course UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S5A, Supporting Information), the increased absorbance over the 300–600 nm region upon increasing the electrolysis time indicates the carbon network formation during the synthesis with TBAPF6 as an electrolyte. Compared to the use of NaCl and (NH4)2SO4 as an electrolyte, ionic liquid TBAPF6 allows the synthesis of CPDs with a higher yield (Fig. S5B, Supporting Information). Taken together, the TBAPF6 electrolysis system not only improved the electrolysis efficiency but also prolonged the stability of the electrode.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the final o-PD CPDs product solution are shown in Fig. 1
A and 1B, respectively. The o-PD CPDs show three absorption bands at 242, 280, and 420 nm (Fig. 1 A). The bands at 420 nm are assigned for the n → π* transition of CN group on the surface of o-PD CPDs [42], and the bands at 242 and 280 nm in UV region are ascribed to the π → π* transition of aromatic CC bonds of carbon network in the core structures [46,47]. The strong and broad absorption from 380 to 500 nm imparts a dark yellow color to the o-PD CPDs solution (inset to Fig. S5A). The fluorescence spectra of o-PD CPDs show two characteristic emissions at 375 and 575 nm (Fig. 1 B), which were not observed in o-PD ( Fig. S6 , Supporting Information). The fluorescence spectra show excitation-dependent emission in the near UV region (360–400 nm) when the excitation wavelength is shorter than 360 nm; however, the emission peak remained constant (575 nm) as the excitation wavelength spans to a longer range. Besides, the yellow color emission reaches its maximum intensity when the excitation wavelength is at 420 nm. We speculate the near UV emission mainly originated from the carbon core state with various CC conjugates [48,49]. The excitation-independent emission property of o-PD CPDs at 575 nm is probably due to the uniform surface microstructures of the CPDs [50–52]. Also, the emission peak at 575 nm is similar to poly-o-PD, supporting the presence of polymer structure on the surface of CPDs [42,53,54].

To explore the fine surface structures of o-PD CPDs, we employed matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization/time of flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) for the analysis of o-PD CPDs. In Fig. S7 (Supporting Information), we inferred the peaks at mass-to-charge (m/z) of 212.2, 317.2, 423.3, 525.3, and 630.5 to be the fragmented o-PD oligomers from dimers to hexamers, respectively, supporting the fact that abundant polymeric structures are present on the surface of o-PD CPDs [43]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of o-PD CPDs at 575 nm was determined to be 5.5 %, which is higher than the reported QY of o-PD CDs synthesized by the electrochemical method in 0.1 M NaCl solution (0.47 %) [35]. The higher-degree carbonized products with insignificant fluorescence are retained in the organic phase, while the hydrophilic o-PD CPDs with strong emission are in the aqueous phase; the two-phase synthesis system allows simple purification besides providing greater production yield. Although hydrothermal methods with high power consumption allow the preparation of CDs with higher QYs (>10 %) [54–56], the electrochemical method under mild conditions avoids over carbonization and thus usually leads to great preserved functional groups of the precursor.

The sizes of o-PD CPDs were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to be 24.6 ± 6.8 nm and 18.3 ± 4.0 nm, respectively, from 50 counts for each measurement (Fig. 1 C and 1D). To further confirm the morphology and size distribution of the o-PD CPDs, atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed. In Fig. S8 (Supporting Information), AFM images show a spherical morphology of the o-PD CPDs with a size distribution of 24.8 ± 4.2 nm. The differences in the measured sizes of CPDs can be attributed to different depositions of the polymeric feature of o-PD CPDs on TEM, SEM, and AFM substrates. In high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1 E), the lattice fringes identified with an interplanar spacing of 0.30 nm corresponds to (002) graphitic plane, which is slightly smaller than graphitic planes (0.34–0.36 nm), due to the edge COC bond between the plane structures [57]. Based on the fluorescence spectra, MALDI-TOF MS, and TEM image, it can be deduced that o-PD CPDs consist of nanosized graphitic sp2 carbon core and polymers anchored surface.

Phenylenediamine-based CDs have shown superior activities in scavenging multiple free radicals [58], but their photosensitizing properties have not been reported. To test whether o-PD CPDs can act as a photosensitizer to produce ROS under irradiation, oxidation of Amplex Red (AR) was performed to demonstrate the photo-induced generation of ROS by o-PD CPDs [59]. As shown in Fig. S9 (Supporting Information), fluorescent resorufin (an oxidation product of AR) was detected during the irradiation of o-PD CPDs by white LED. It is noteworthy that o-PD CPDs exposed under light without AR showed significant quenching of fluorescence at 575 nm (Fig. 2
A). Furthermore, a similar fluorescence quenching of o-PD CPDs was also observed upon irradiation with white LED even in physiological-mimicking buffer (PBS) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) ( Fig. S10
C, Supporting Information), which demonstrates that o-PD CPDs can be used in biological systems for sensing ROS. Like N-doped CDs [60–62], we believe that the smaller inter-system crossing energy (graphitic N) and numerous oxygen adsorption site (pyrrolic N) in the o-PD CPDs are responsible for the interesting photosensitizing properties.

The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was employed to characterize the ROS generation by o-PD CPDs using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TMP) as a free-radical trapper. As illustrated in Fig. 2 B (blue and pink curves), a characteristic 1O2-induced signal due to the formation of 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1‑oxyl (TMPO) was observed in the ESR spectra only under white LED irradiation [63]. The results suggest that the energy transfer from the o-PD CPDs to oxygen in the ground-state result in the generation of 1O2 [64]. The catalytic formation of 1O2 by o-PD CPDs was further confirmed by conducting 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) assay ( Fig. S11 , Supporting Information) [16,65]. DPBF selectively reacted with the 1O2 generated by the o-PD CPDs upon irradiation to form a colorless adduct (1,2-dibenzoylbenzene, DBB), as evident from the decrease in the absorption band at 410 nm [16].

The catalytic quantum yield (Φ) of 1O2 generation by o-PD CPDs under white LED irradiation was determined by using 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA) as a trapping agent and Rose bengal (RB) as a standard photosensitizer (1O2 quantum yield ΦRB = 0.75 in water). As shown in Fig. S12 , the absorbance of ABDA at 378 nm decreased gradually during the irradiation, indicating the degradation of ABDA by 1O2 generated by RB or o-PD CPDs. The decomposition rate constants (K D) of ABDA by RB and o-PD CPDs were calculated to be 0.111 and 0.027, respectively. The catalytic quantum yield of 1O2 generation by o-PD CPDs was estimated to be 0.15. Moreover, a noticeable decrease in the white-light LED irradiation-induced fluorescence quenching of the o-PD CPDs was observed in the presence of sodium azide (NaN3), a specific 1O2 scavenger ( Fig. S13 , Supporting Information). Notably, the quenching rate of the o-PD CPDs correlates to the NaN3 concentration, implying that the fluorescence quenching of o-PD CPDs is due to the in situ generated 1O2 under the irradiation of white LED. We further used D2O to prolong the lifetime of 1O2 and measured the fluorescence change of o-PD CPDs after the irradiation by LED light. The purified o-PD CPDs (20 μg mL−1) were prepared in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) in D2O, and then were irradiated with a white LED light (60 mW cm−2) for different periods of time (0–30 min). In D2O, the o-PD CPDs exhibited a higher quenching rate (85 %) after 6-min irradiation than that in H2O (40 %), which verified the contribution of 1O2 in the FL quenching process of o-PD CPDs under light irradiation ( Fig. S14 ).

To prove there is no accumulation of positive charges (holes) in the fluorescence quenching process of o-PD CPDs under light irradiation, we utilized the hole scavenger, 4‑methylbenzyl alcohol (4-MBA), to prevent their possible accumulation, in the following experiment [66]. The purified o-PD CPDs (20 μg mL−1) were prepared in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), followed by the addition of 10 mM 4-MBA (>99.0 % GC, Tokyo Chemical Industry, Tokyo, Japan), and then were irradiated with a white-light-emitting diode (LED) plate (60 mW cm−2) for 10 min. However, in the presence of 4-MBA, o-PD CPDs showed no significant difference in fluorescence quenching after white-light irradiation compared to that without 4-MBA. Therefore, we can exclude the existence of positive charge (hole) accumulation during the oxidative degradation of o-PD CPDs.

After irradiating the o-PD CPDs with white-light LED, aggregates with sizes > 150 nm were formed, which tend to form a cluster structure (>1 μm) after being deposited on carbon-coated copper grid (for TEM) or silicon wafer (for SEM) substrate (Fig. 2 C and 2D). The DLS measurements of o-PD CPDs in sodium phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) were in accordance with the TEM and SEM results, which show an increase in the average hydrodynamic diameter from 16.5 ± 5.6 to 229.0 ± 62.7 nm ( Fig. S15
A, Supporting Information). The zeta potentials of o-PD CPDs before and after white LED irradiation were found to be −13.9 ± 8.4 and −39.9 ± 15.1 mV, respectively ( Fig. S15
B). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra show three peaks at 284.4, 399.0 and 531.9 eV ( Fig. S16 , Supporting Information), which are attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The XPS elemental ratio analysis shows an increased oxygen content in o-PD CPDs from 16 to 23 %, indicating the oxidation of o-PD CPDs by the in situ generated 1O2 during white LED irradiation. The oxidation of o-PD CPDs forms a higher number of oxygen-containing functional groups, leading to an increase in zeta potential (more negative) but a decrease in fluorescence intensity [67,68]. The deconvolution of C 1s spectra shows the existence of CC/CC (284.2 eV), CN (285.2 eV), CO/CN (286.0 eV), and O-CO (288.2 eV) bonds on the o-PD CPDs. The decrease in the intensity of deconvoluted CC/CC peak after oxidation is mainly due to the [4 + 2] cycloaddition of the aromatic CC in o-PD CPDs with 1O2, followed by the formation of oxygen-containing groups (e.g, CO and C-OH) [69,70]. An increase in CN peak intensity was observed after irradiation due to the 1O2-mediated reaction as a result of imine production [71,72]. The 1O2 can stimulate the oxidative coupling reaction in that the oxidated primary amine is changed into the electrophilic imine followed by the nucleophilic addition to attack the other adjacent amine group to form linkages. The O 1s spectrum of o-PD CPDs shows two distinct peaks at 531.6 and 532.4 eV, ascribed to CO and C-OH functional groups, respectively, revealing oxygen doping into the o-PD CPDs during the synthesis. The intensity of CO decreased after irradiation, implying a nucleophilic addition of amines to form imine [73]. The deconvolution of N 1s spectra shows the existence of four types of nitrogen bindings, ascribed to pyridinic N (398.6 eV), 1°/2° amine (399.2 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV) and graphitic N (401.0 eV) bonds. After irradiation, a slight decrease in the amino groups further indicates their reaction with carbonyl groups to form imine bonds.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements further verified the formation of the imine bonds, with evidence of a vibrational band at 1620 cm−1 ( Fig. S17 , Supporting Information). The broad peaks at 3340, 3100 and 2920 cm−1 are attributed to OH, NH and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. The other four characteristic peaks at 1750, 1500, 1220 and 1050 cm−1 are assigned for the stretching vibration of CO, CC, CN and COC groups on the surface of the o-PD CPDs, respectively. Besides, the result also proved the existence of oxygen and amino groups in the o-PD CPDs. We also conducted solid-state NMR (ss NMR) for the analysis of the o-PD CPDs after being reacted with 1O2. The 13C NMR spectrum of the main polymeric structure on the surface of o-PD CPDs reveals the existence of phenazine-like units (blue curve in Fig. S18 , Supporting Information). The 13C chemical shift (δ) values of104.4, 128.3, and 141.7 are referred to c, b, and a site carbon, respectively [74]. Two peaks at 171.3 and 82.1 that are assigned for carboxylic and endoperoxide functional groups, respectively, emerged after the white-light LED irradiation for 60 min (green curve), further supporting the formation of [4 + 2] oxidation product induced by 1O2 [75]. Besides, we also employed MALDI-TOF MS to analyze the surface structures of o-PD CPDs after 1O2 induced its oxidation and aggregation. As shown in Fig. S19 (Supporting Information), we observed obvious oxidation signals (Δm/z ∼ 16 and 32) in the oligomer peaks (dimers to octamers) of the o-PD CPDs, supporting the [4 + 2] cycloaddition reaction of the 1O2 with the polymeric o-PD on the CPDs.

In summary, white LED irradiation induced the in situ generated 1O2 to oxidize the surface functional groups of o-PD CPDs, causing the fluorescence quenching of surface states. Then the intrinsic primary amino groups attack the newly-formed carbonyl groups through the nucleophilic addition followed by the elimination of H2O to form imines (a Schiff base) or undergo 1O2-mediated oxidative coupling reaction to form imines. The imination reaction causes the crosslinking that results in the aggregation of o-PD CPDs ( Fig. S20 , Supporting Information).

Based on the fact that the fluorescence intensity of the o-PD CPDs decreases in the presence of catalytically generated 1O2, we employed o-PD CPDs as a fluorescence probe to detect 1O2 generated separately from RB/green-light irradiation and H2O2/NaClO. The fluorescence intensity at 575 nm of o-PD CPDs decreased in the presence of RB exposed to a green-light irradiation (535 nm, 20 mW cm−2) in Fig. S21
A (Supporting Information). Similarly, fluorescence quenching was also observed for o-PD CPDs in the presence of 1O2 produced by H2O2/NaClO ( Fig. S21
B) [76]. By contrast, the emission peak of o-PD CPDs at 375 nm remains constant, revealing 1O2 cannot affect the core emission. Therefore, o-PD CPDs hold great potential as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of 1O2, irrespective of the source of generation. Taking the emission intensity at 375 nm as an internal standard, the ratiometric fluorescence intensities [(I F0 575/375-I F 575/375)/I F0 575/375] of o-PD CPDs were found to be correlated with the concentrations of 1O2 (Fig. 3 A and 3B). A linear response was obtained over the 1O2 concentrations ranging from 12.5 to 500 μM (R 2 = 0.980). The ratiometric method provided a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 μM with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 for 1O2. Compared to reported organic probes such as SOSG, DPBF, ABDA, anthracene- and heteroarenes-based molecules for sensing 1O2 [1,77], our o-PD CPDs have greater water dispersibility and cell permeation efficiency favorable for monitoring intracellular 1O2. Relative to other nanoparticle-based sensors, the o-PD CPDs could be prepared in a fast and one-step convenient electrolysis process [26,27]. With the advantages of simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and rapidity, the electrolysis method is powerful for the preparation of carbonized nanomaterials as sensitive probes for ROS detection.

To test the selectivity of o-PD CPDs for 1O2, the fluorescence of o-PD CPDs in the presence of other ROS species such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hypochlorite (ClO-), hydroxyl radical (⋅OH), and superoxide (⋅O2 –) (each at 2.0 mM) were determined (Fig. 3 C and 3D). The results reveal their negligible effect in quenching the fluorescence at 575 nm, except for ClO- that significantly quenches the fluorescence of the o-PD CPDs at 575 and 375 nm. The quenching of the emission at both wavelengths probably resulted from the strong oxidation power of ClO- that could easily oxidize both shell and core structures of o-PD CPDs [78,79]. It is thus easy to differentiate ClO- from 1O2 using our o-PD CPD probe by simply comparing their fluoresce quenching effects at the wavelengths of 375 and 575 nm. With great selectivity and sensitivity, the ratiometric o-PD CPDs probe was further employed for the quantitation of 1O2 in the complex biological system.

Firstly, the biocompatibility of o-PD CPDs toward MCF-7 cells was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S22 (Supporting Information), 90 % cell viability was observed when the concentrations of o-PD CPDs were up to 50 μg mL−1 and the viability was 60 % at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1. Thus, the o-PD CPDs (50 μg mL−1) were used for sensing cellular 1O2. The bioimaging of MCF-7 cells was carried out by phase-contrast fluorescence microscopy after incubating the cells with o-PD CPDs for 4 h (control group). As shown in Fig. 4
A, the cells show strong blue and yellow fluorescence intensities, indicating o-PD CPDs were high cell-permeant. The dual-channel emissions originated from the carbon core structures and polymeric shells of o-PD CPDs, respectively. As mentioned in the previous section, the 1O2 is generated by adding a high concentration of H2O2 (2.0 mM) followed by the continuous addition of NaClO. Upon the addition of NaClO, the yellow fluorescence is gradually quenched by the generated 1O2; meanwhile, the blue fluorescence remains constant as shown in the PBS solution. Notably, when the amount of NaClO (2.2 mM) is higher than that of H2O2 (2 mM), the blue fluorescence disappears dramatically due to the complete oxidation of o-PD CPDs by an excessive NaClO. A prominent trend of color change (yellow/blue ratio) in the fluorescence image from violet to blue was observed upon increasing intracellular 1O2 level. Fig. 4 B reveals the fluorescence ratio of yellow-to-blue is well correlated to the intracellular concentration of 1O2.

Furthermore, we evaluated the endogenous release of 1O2 in the neutrophil cells taken from rat blood. Neutrophils are involved as mediators of inflammation [80]. In addition, reports show the importance of 1O2 species in inducing inflammation reactions in neutrophil cells, and 1O2 has been proven to be directly related to neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation that enables the cells to capture the external bacteria at the inflammatory stage [81,82]. Even in patients with chronic granulomatous disease, an inherited immunodeficiency with NADPH oxidase defect, the administration of 1O2 can induce the NET formation of neutrophil cells as in healthy people. Therefore, in our study, inflammation was induced in neutrophil cells by the addition of phorbol myristate acetate (PMA), a well-known neutrophil activator [83]. In brief, after being treated with o-PD CPDs, the neutrophil cells exhibited a bright dual-color fluorescence as shown in Fig. 5 A. Upon PMA-induced inflammation, both of the blue and yellow fluorescences were gradually quenched and almost vanished when 50 nM of PMA was added. In Fig. 5 B, the fluorescence intensities of neutrophil cells were measured accordingly, showing that both fluorescences were readily quenched in PMA-stimulated neutrophil cells. As previously demonstrated, the yellow fluorescence of the o-PD CPDs was responsive to 1O2, while the blue fluorescence was not. The observed dual fluorescence quenching (blue and yellow) in PMA-stimulated neutrophil cells might be attributed to the production of other ROS in addition to 1O2, such as HOCl that can strongly oxidize both shell and core structures of o-PD CPDs, resulting in dual fluorescence quenching of the o-PD CPDs.

In summary, we prepared water-soluble o-PD CPDs in a two-phase solution by applying a simple electrochemical method. Due to the long π-conjugation in carbonized core and polymer-attached surface, o-PD CPDs possess dual-emissive property. o-PD CPDs also exhibit photosensitizing features to generate 1O2 under white LED irradiation. The surface-emission (575 nm) of o-PD CPDs is highly selective toward 1O2, whereas the core emission (375 nm) is not, allowing for the quantitation of 1O2 by conducting a ratiometric fluorescence approach. With good biocompatibility and membrane permeability, o-PD CPDs were successfully employed to monitor real-time 1O2 levels in MCF-7 cells. Besides, the o-PD CPDs could also be operated in the neutrophil cell system to detect PMA-induced inflammation. The o-PD CPDs with high sensitivity and selectivity toward 1O2 hold great potential for sensing or bioimaging of 1O2 in many biological systems.
